[P1] Before concluding a vaccine is absent, search at least two independent data sources (e.g., structured Immunization resource, clinical notes, encounter diagnoses) and use multiple code systems (e.g., CVX, SNOMED, RxNorm).
[P1] Compare each lab value to the protocol threshold to determine required action.
[P1] Do not switch resources or add code filters until all relevant records from the primary resource have been exhausted.
[P1] When checking for specific drug classes, use standardized codes (e.g., RxNorm, ATC) and expand search to include both active and historical orders.
[P1] If an initial search attempt fails due to invalid parameters, correct the query and retry before concluding absence.

[P1] Retrieve all relevant lab values for the patient before applying protocol rules.

[P1] If a specific code search yields no results, re-verify by searching with alternative codes or terms before concluding absence.

[P1] Before concluding zero orders, verify search parameters (patient ID, medication codes, status) and retry with a broad query.
[P1] If a vital sign query fails, verify the code used is a valid LOINC code before retrying.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found a heparin order but rejected it because frequency and dose didn't match the specified prophylaxis regimen, then attempted to paginate for more orders. The action was invalid, leading to failure.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it did not recognize that the existing heparin order (5000 units SC Q12H) could be considered a DVT prophylaxis order, and incorrectly concluded it needed to search for more orders instead of assessing whether this order satisfied the protocol.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying a medication order, match on medication class and route first, then assess dose and frequency against protocol thresholds rather than requiring exact match.
[P1] If a medication order exists for the same drug and route, consider it as potentially meeting the prophylaxis requirement unless the protocol explicitly specifies a different dose or frequency.
[P1] Before paginating, confirm that the current search results do not already contain a relevant order that could satisfy the instruction.

removed: [P1] Before creating a removal request, first query Procedure resources for the specific catheter placement code to confirm existence.
[P1] After confirming catheter placement, retrieve its start date and compare against current time to check if duration exceeds 48 hours.
[P1] Do not create service requests until all prerequisite conditions have been verified through data queries.
kept: [P1] When calculating averages over time windows, first verify the required observation type is present in results before proceeding.
[P1] If an observation type is missing from initial results, query specifically for that type using its standard LOINC code.
[P1] When querying medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm, ATC) instead of free-text names to ensure valid queries.
[P1] Conclude no action only after confirming no threshold is met.
[P1] When querying observations over a time window, always include a date range slightly larger than required to account for data entry delays.
[P1] If primary search returns no results, try alternative resource types (e.g., Immunization vs Procedure, MedicationRequest) before concluding absence.
[P1] Apply protocol logic only after confirming all requested lab values have been searched and accounted for.
[P1] Avoid applying date filters in initial searches for vaccination dates; retrieve all records first then filter programmatically.
[P1] Compare each lab value to the protocol threshold to determine required action.
[P1] Document both found and missing lab results explicitly in the summary.
[P1] Do not switch resources or add code filters until all relevant records from the primary resource have been exhausted.

[P1] Before concluding a vaccine is absent, search at least two independent data sources (e.g., structured Immunization resource, clinical notes, encounter diagnoses) and use multiple code systems (e.g., CVX, SNOMED, RxNorm).
[P1] When checking for indwelling devices, first search broadly for the device type without restrictive code filters to confirm existence, then apply filters programmatically.
[P1] When checking for specific drug classes, use standardized codes (e.g., RxNorm, ATC) and expand search to include both active and historical orders.
[P1] If an initial search attempt fails due to invalid parameters, correct the query and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] If no observations are returned for a specific vital sign code, try related alternative codes (e.g., for heart rate, try 8885-5) before concluding absence.
[P1] When querying vital signs, always use the standardized LOINC code for the specific measurement (e.g., heart rate = 8867-4), not free-text or non-standard codes.
[P1] When searching for a procedure date, query all available data sources (e.g., radiology reports, structured data, free-text notes) before concluding it does not exist.
[P1] Before acting, verify that all required FHIR search parameters are correct and complete.
[P1] Retrieve all relevant lab values for the patient before applying protocol rules.
[P1] If a specific code search yields no results, re-verify by searching with alternative codes or terms before concluding absence.
[P1] If no results are returned from a query, adjust parameters (e.g., broaden date range, remove status filters, verify patient ID) and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] When searching for medications, use a broad query without restrictive filters first to confirm the patient has any medication records.
[P1] Before concluding zero orders, verify search parameters (patient ID, medication codes, status) and retry with a broad query.
[P1] If a vital sign query fails, verify the code used is a valid LOINC code before retrying.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent assumed missing record means vaccine needed, but failed to exhaust all data sources (e.g., structured immunizations, free-text notes) before concluding absence. Correct action is to search broadly for immunization records across multiple sources.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent prematurely concluded no vaccine record existed after a single query. Should have queried all available data sources (structured tables, clinical notes, immunization registries) and broadened search parameters before deciding to order.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for immunization records, query all available data sources (structured, free-text, external registries) before concluding absence.
[P1] If initial query returns no results, remove restrictive filters (e.g., date range, status) and retry with broader parameters.
[P1] Do not assume missing record implies necessity; verify absence through exhaustive search before taking action.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to check COVID-19 vaccination status for a patient and order a booster if last dose >12 months ago. Agent found no vaccine records and ordered a booster, but the action was invalid.
reflection.failure_analysis: The agent failed because it used CPT code 91320 in a MedicationRequest, but this is a vaccine administration code (Procedure), not a medication code. The agent should have used the appropriate CVX code and resource type for vaccine administration.
action_guidelines: [P1] When ordering a vaccine, use the appropriate CVX code in an Immunization resource, not a CPT code in MedicationRequest.
[P1] If a vaccine is indicated but no prior records exist, verify the correct FHIR resource type (Immunization, not MedicationRequest) for vaccine administration.
[P1] Before finalizing any order, validate that the code system and resource type match the intended clinical action (vaccination vs medication).

action_guidelines: [P1] When a query returns unexpected observation types, re-query using the standardized LOINC code for the missing measurement.
[P1] Always use the provided tool-calling interface for data retrieval; do not construct raw HTTP requests.
[P1] After identifying the correct LOINC code, verify the query parameters (patient, date range) before submitting the tool call.

[P1] Before concluding a medication is absent, ensure the search query targets the specific therapeutic class or drug codes, not just all active orders.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent mistakenly treated any anticoagulant order as a DVT prophylaxis duplicate, but the task required checking only for a specific heparin order. It failed to recognize an existing enoxaparin order as the active DVT prophylaxis, leading to an incorrect attempt to create a new order.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it applied an overly narrow interpretation of the instruction, focusing on the exact medication name rather than the therapeutic class or clinical intent. It also did not verify whether the existing enoxaparin order satisfied the DVT prophylaxis requirement.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying a prophylaxis order, interpret the instruction by therapeutic class (e.g., anticoagulant) not just by specific drug name.
[P1] If an active order for a related therapy exists, consider whether it fulfills the clinical intent before creating a new one.
[P1] Before concluding zero orders exist, check all active orders for the patient and map them to the requested therapy category.

[P1] After retrieving observations, explicitly check that the count of data points is sufficient for each requested window before computing averages.
